title: How a Model Actually Gets "Trained"
concept: Training a model is a repeating loop: make a guess, check how wrong it was, nudge the guess to be less wrong, and repeat thousands of times until the guesses are good enough.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A model that scores perfectly on training data but poorly on new data is a healthy, well-trained model.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False — you learned that's a sign of overfitting. Today we go deeper: what is actually happening, step by step, while a model trains in the first place?

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do we test a model on data it has never seen (like you learned with train vs. test data)?

options.text: To see if it truly learned a pattern, not just memorized examples
Because training data runs out of storage space
Testing isn't actually necessary if training went well

exercises.explain: Right! Now let's zoom into training itself — the process that happens *before* any testing even starts.

cards.title: Learning to shoot free throws
cards.body: Mia's first free throw airballs completely. She notices it fell short, so she pushes a little harder next time. That one sails long, so she eases up slightly. Shot after shot, her aim keeps tightening — she's not guessing randomly anymore, she's adjusting based on what just happened.

cards.title: What is the training loop?
cards.body: Training a model means repeating four steps over and over: 1) start with a guess, 2) make a prediction, 3) measure how wrong it was, 4) nudge the guess to reduce that error — then repeat, often thousands of times, until the predictions are good enough.

cards.title: The model is Mia at the free-throw line
cards.body: Initialize = Mia's very first, untrained attempt. Predict = each shot she takes. Measure error = noticing the ball landed short or long. Adjust = correcting her arm angle. Repeat = shot after shot after shot, getting closer to the hoop each time.

cards.title: A lemonade-price bot
cards.body: A bot starts by guessing a random price: $3. It sells almost nothing (way too high), so it lowers the price. Next day it sells out fast (too cheap, left money on the table), so it raises the price slightly. After many days of this predict-measure-adjust cycle, it settles near the price that sells the most cups for the most profit.

cards.title: Training isn't one magic step
cards.body: It's tempting to picture training as the computer "instantly figuring it out." In reality, a model's very first guesses are usually bad — even random. Good performance only shows up after many, many rounds of predict → check error → adjust.

cards.title: One pass vs. the training loop
cards.body: Why repetition is the whole point:

left.text:
Guess once, keep it forever
Usually stays wildly wrong

right.text:
Guess → check error → adjust
Repeated until good enough

exercises.prompt: What is the very first step in the training loop, before a model has learned anything?

options.text: It starts with an initial guess — often random or arbitrary
It starts by already knowing the correct answer for every case
It starts by deleting all the training data
It starts by testing on brand-new data first

exercises.explain: Every training loop begins with some starting guess, which is usually far from correct — that's expected and normal.

exercises.prompt: True or false: After a model makes a prediction, the next step is to measure how wrong that prediction was.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Measuring the error is what tells the model which direction to adjust in next.

exercises.sentence: Training is a loop of predict, measure error, and ___ — repeated many times.
exercises.explain: After measuring the error, the model nudges its guess to reduce that error, then predicts again.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes what's happening during model training?

options.text: A weather model keeps adjusting its rules after each day it predicts wrong, getting more accurate over time
A weather model is given the exact answer key and never changes anything
A weather model makes one prediction and is immediately finished forever

exercises.explain: Correct! Real training involves repeated cycles of predicting, checking error, and adjusting — not a single one-shot guess.

exercises.prompt: A model's early training predictions are usually way off. What does this tell you?

options.text: That's normal — the model hasn't had enough rounds of adjustment yet
The model is broken and should be thrown out immediately
The model has already overfit to the training data
Training must have already finished

exercises.explain: Bad early guesses are expected — that's exactly why the loop repeats so many times, tightening the aim like Mia's free throws.

exercises.prompt: Put these training loop steps in the order they actually happen: (1) adjust the guess, (2) make a prediction, (3) start with an initial guess, (4) measure the error.

options.text: 3 → 2 → 4 → 1, then repeat
1 → 3 → 2 → 4, then stop
4 → 2 → 1 → 3, then stop
2 → 4 → 3 → 1, then repeat

exercises.explain: Exactly! Initialize, predict, measure error, adjust — then loop back to predicting again with the improved guess.

exercises.prompt: A robot arm is learning to stack blocks. On attempt 1 it knocks the tower over immediately. By attempt 50, it stacks blocks smoothly almost every time. What best explains this improvement?

options.text: Across 50 attempts, it repeatedly predicted a move, saw how much it missed by, and adjusted — the training loop in action
It got lucky on attempt 50 with no connection to the earlier attempts
Someone manually reprogrammed it between every single attempt
It memorized the exact same tower it will be tested on later

exercises.explain: Right! Steady improvement across many attempts is the signature of a training loop nudging the guess closer each round.
